title: Performance Management
simple_html:
Performance management is the ongoing process of making sure employees' work supports the organization's goals — by setting clear expectations, giving feedback, developing people and recognizing results. It is far more than the once-a-year appraisal form.

The cycle

Think of it as a continuous loop: plan (agree goals and standards), act and monitor (do the work, give ongoing feedback and coaching), review (assess how it went), and reward/develop (recognize achievement and plan improvement). Then the cycle repeats.

Setting good goals

Goals work best when they are SMART — Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound. Clear, challenging goals lift performance because people know what "good" looks like and can track progress.

Feedback and review

Regular feedback beats a single annual verdict. Many organizations now use continuous check-ins instead of, or alongside, the formal appraisal. 360-degree feedback gathers views from a manager, peers, direct reports and sometimes customers for a rounded picture.

Key takeaways

  • Performance management is a continuous cycle, not just an annual form.
  • Set SMART, aligned goals so effort points at organizational priorities.
  • Give frequent, specific feedback and coaching.
  • Separate (or carefully link) development conversations from pay decisions.
  • Fair, well-run reviews build trust; biased or box-ticking ones destroy it.
intermediate_html:
Performance management (PM) is a continuous, integrated process through which managers and employees align individual contribution with organizational objectives, develop capability and recognize results. This lesson covers the PM cycle, goal-setting theory, appraisal methods and their pitfalls, the shift to continuous performance management, and how to link PM to development and reward — with a worked example.

1. Performance management vs appraisal

A common error is equating PM with the annual appraisal. Appraisal is a periodic assessment event; PM is the wider, ongoing system of planning, monitoring, developing and rewarding performance throughout the year (Aguinis, 2019). Effective PM is strategic (linked to goals), integrated (joined to other HR practices) and continuous.

2. The PM cycle

A standard cycle has four stages: (1) Planning — agree objectives and the behaviours/competencies expected; (2) Acting & monitoring — perform, with ongoing feedback, coaching and adjustment; (3) Reviewing — formally assess results and behaviours; (4) Rewarding & developing — recognize achievement, address gaps and feed into pay and L&D. The loop then restarts, ideally cascading organizational goals down to teams and individuals (line of sight).

3. Goal-setting

Goal-setting theory (Locke & Latham, 1990) is the most robust foundation: specific and challenging goals, when accepted and supported by feedback, produce higher performance than vague "do your best" goals. The popular SMART criteria (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) operationalize this. OKRs (Objectives and Key Results) and the balanced scorecard (Kaplan & Norton, 1992) are tools for cascading and balancing goals across financial, customer, process and learning dimensions.

4. Appraisal methods and rating errors

Methods include rating scales (often behaviourally anchored — BARS), management by objectives (MBO), narrative/essay, forced ranking/distribution, and 360-degree feedback (multi-rater input from manager, peers, reports and customers — best for development, riskier for pay). Raters are prone to systematic errors: halo/horns, leniency/strictness, central tendency, recency, similar-to-me and contrast effects. Remedies include behavioural anchors, rater training (frame-of-reference training), multiple raters and documentation.

5. The shift to continuous PM

Many organizations have moved away from annual ratings toward continuous performance management — frequent check-ins, real-time feedback and forward-looking coaching — because annual reviews are infrequent, backward-looking and often demotivating. The aim is to make feedback timely and developmental, though pay decisions still require some periodic summary judgement.

6. Worked example

A SaaS company replaces annual appraisals with quarterly OKRs plus monthly one-to-ones. A customer-success manager's objective "improve retention" becomes the SMART key result "raise net revenue retention from 95% to 102% by Q4." Monthly check-ins surface a blocker (slow onboarding) early, prompting a coaching action and a process fix. At review, the manager assesses both the result (102.5% — met) and behaviours (collaboration with product). Because ratings here also influence the bonus, the firm uses calibration meetings across managers to curb leniency and ensure consistency before pay is decided.

7. How to apply it

  • Design PM as a year-round cycle, not a form; cascade goals for line of sight.
  • Write SMART, accepted, appropriately challenging goals and revisit them.
  • Assess both results and behaviours; use BARS and rater training to reduce bias.
  • Use calibration when ratings drive pay; favour continuous feedback for development.
  • Close the loop to L&D (build ability) and reward (recognize motivation) — the AMO levers.

This lesson operationalizes the Motivation lever of AMO (Lesson 2) and links tightly to Reward Management (Lesson 6) and Learning & Development (Lesson 5).
full_html:
Performance management (PM) is the continuous, strategic and integrated process directed at improving organizational performance by developing the performance of individuals and teams and aligning it with organizational goals (Armstrong, 2017; Aguinis, 2019). It is among the most consequential and most criticized HR systems, sitting at the intersection of motivation, measurement, development and reward. This lesson develops its theory, the cycle, measurement methods and errors, the contemporary continuous-PM movement, the reward linkage debate, and a worked case, with seminal citations and common pitfalls.

1. Definition and purposes

PM serves multiple, sometimes conflicting, purposes: strategic (aligning behaviour with goals), administrative (informing pay, promotion, retention decisions), developmental (feedback and growth), documentation (legal defensibility), and communication/maintenance of the performance culture (Aguinis, 2019). The conflict between the developmental and evaluative/administrative purposes is foundational: employees are unlikely to discuss weaknesses candidly in the same conversation that determines their pay — a tension that shapes the whole system's design.

2. Theoretical foundations

Several theories underpin PM. Goal-setting theory (Locke & Latham, 1990) establishes that specific, challenging, accepted goals with feedback raise performance via direction, effort, persistence and strategy. Expectancy theory (Vroom, 1964) explains motivation as a product of expectancy (effort→performance), instrumentality (performance→reward) and valence (value of reward) — PM must make all three credible. Equity theory (Adams, 1965) warns that perceived unfairness in effort–reward ratios destroys motivation, foregrounding organizational justice — distributive, procedural and interactional fairness (Greenberg, 1987) — as the key to acceptance. Control theory frames PM as feedback loops comparing performance to standards.

3. The performance management cycle

The canonical cycle is continuous: plan (goals and competencies, cascaded from strategy for "line of sight"), act/monitor (ongoing feedback, coaching, support), review (assessment of results and behaviours), and reward/develop (recognition, pay linkage, development planning), then iterate. Goal cascading tools include the balanced scorecard (Kaplan & Norton, 1992), which balances financial, customer, internal-process and learning-and-growth measures, and OKRs, which pair aspirational objectives with measurable key results.

4. Measurement: what and how

PM measures both what is achieved (results/outputs) and how (behaviours/competencies) — measuring results alone invites gaming and unethical behaviour; measuring behaviours alone risks activity without outcomes. Methods include: rating scales and especially behaviourally anchored rating scales (BARS; Smith & Kendall, 1963) which tie ratings to concrete behavioural examples; management by objectives (Drucker, 1954); narrative approaches; comparative methods (ranking, paired comparison, forced distribution); and multi-source 360-degree feedback. 360 is well suited to development but problematic for administrative decisions because of rater motivation and reliability concerns.

5. Rating errors and the measurement problem

Human raters introduce systematic error: halo/horns, leniency, strictness, central tendency, recency, primacy, similar-to-me and contrast effects, plus deliberate politics (Murphy & Cleveland, 1995, show ratings are partly motivated social judgements, not pure measurement). Mitigations: BARS, frame-of-reference training, multiple raters, calibration sessions across managers, and documentation. The deeper critique (Deming and the quality movement) argues that much performance variation is due to the system, not the individual, so ranking individuals against each other is statistically unsound and corrosive — a key argument behind rating-free approaches.

6. The forced-distribution debate and the continuous-PM turn

Forced ranking ("rank-and-yank," associated with GE under Welch) was widely adopted then largely abandoned because it damages collaboration, morale and accuracy. More broadly, from the mid-2010s many large firms (Adobe, Deloitte, GE, Microsoft) abolished or de-emphasized annual ratings in favour of continuous performance management: frequent check-ins, real-time and forward-looking feedback, and coaching. Evidence and practitioner reviews (e.g., CEB/Gartner; Pulakos & O'Leary, 2011, who argue PM "can be fixed" by focusing on the manager–employee relationship) suggest the quality of ongoing conversations matters more than the form. Yet abolishing ratings creates new problems — pay and promotion still need defensible differentiation, and managers vary in feedback skill — so most settle on a hybrid: continuous feedback plus a lighter periodic summary and calibration.

7. Worked example with justice lens

A bank shifts from a five-point annual rating tied directly to bonus, toward quarterly objectives, monthly coaching and a year-end holistic judgement informed by calibration. An analyst sets the SMART/OKR objective: "reduce report turnaround from 5 to 3 days by Q3 without raising error rate." Monthly check-ins (procedural justice — voice and consistency) catch a data-access bottleneck; the manager removes it (interactional justice — respectful, supportive treatment). At year-end, results (achieved: 2.8 days, errors flat) and behaviours (mentored a junior) are assessed; calibration across the analyst pool curbs one lenient manager's inflation before bonuses (distributive justice). The design deliberately keeps developmental coaching frequent and separate-ish from the single pay moment, honouring the developmental/evaluative tension.

8. Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Equating PM with the appraisal form — the conversations and coaching are the substance.
  • Measuring only results — invites gaming, short-termism and ethics failures.
  • Mixing pay and development in one conversation without managing the tension — candour collapses.
  • Ignoring justice — perceived unfairness (especially procedural) predicts rejection of even accurate ratings.
  • Assuming abolishing ratings solves everything — it shifts the burden to manager capability and pay-differentiation methods.
  • Under-investing in manager skill — the manager–employee relationship is the main moderator of PM effectiveness (Pulakos & O'Leary, 2011).

9. Cross-links

PM is the principal vehicle for the Motivation lever of AMO (Lesson 2). It feeds Reward Management (Lesson 6) through the contested performance–pay link (see Performance & Reward Linkage), and Learning & Development (Lesson 5) through development planning. Its data and validity concerns connect to Talent, Wellbeing & HR Analytics (Lesson 8), and its justice and voice dimensions to Employee Relations & Engagement (Lesson 7). Mastery means designing a justice-grounded, continuous cycle that measures both results and behaviours, manages the developmental–evaluative tension, and is enacted by capable managers.
